---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size60[
Build univariate visualizations in R
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2024)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. 



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size40[.center[**New Script: 2023-02-15-Univariate_Viz.R**]]

.pull-left[
.center[.size30[
**Option 1**

"File" 

&#8595;

"New File" 

&#8595;

"R Script"
]]]

.pull-right[
.center[.size30[**Option 2**]]

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='38%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/03_3-New_Script.png")
```

]

???

Let's get set-up!

- Everybody create a new R script for notes and code on building univariate visualizations.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size60[**ggplot() for making visualizations**]]

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '20%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/03_2-ggplot_logo.png")
```

.center[.size50[**Note: You have to load the tidyverse to use ggplot**]]

```{r, echo=TRUE}
library(tidyverse)
```

???

### Questions on the basics of ggplot as introduced in the Healy reading?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size55[**The GGplot Function: Two Basic Steps**]]

.pull-left[
.center[.size50[
**Step 1**

Map your tidy data
]]
]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '70%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/03_2-Axes.png")
```
]

.code130[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
## GGplot Step 1: Map the Tidy Data
newobject <- ggplot(data = ?, aes(x = ?, y = ?))
```
]

???

Step 1: Tell ggplot the name of the data set and what variable to plot on each axis

- Save this as a new object (example here is 'p' but can be almost anything)




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size55[**The GGplot Function: Two Basic Steps**]]

<br>

.pull-left[
.center[.size50[
**Step 2**

Add a geom
]]
]

.pull-right[
.code140[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# GGplot Step 2
# Add a geom
newobject + geom_bar()
newobject + geom_histogram()
newobject + geom_boxplot()
newobject + geom_line()
```
]
]

???

Step 2, tell ggplot what kind of picture to draw using this data.
- Everybody write these down!

<br>

REMEMBER, data analysis principle 3 is "Variable type determines tool"
- ggplot calls each tool a 'geom'

<br>

Let's practice doing these two steps to remake all the plots you drew by hand last class.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[.content-box-blue[**Categorical Variable: Make a Bar Plot**]]]

.pull-left[
.size55[
**Bar Plots**

1. Counts

2. Proportions
]]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/03_1-Johnson-Bar_plot.png")
```
]

???

Data Visualization 1: A Bar Plot

- When you have a categorical variable and you'd like to count the levels (or calculate the proportions)




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size45[.content-box-blue[**Categorical Variable: Make a Bar Plot**]]

<br>

.size45[**In R** make a bar plot of drive train levels (`drv`) for the 234 cars in the `mpg` dataset.]]

<br>

.code150[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
## GGplot Step 1: Map the Tidy Data
plot1 <- ggplot(data = mpg, aes(x = drv))
```
]

???

We'll start where we did last time by building a bar plot that counts the levels of the drive trains in the cars of the mpg data set.

Here I'm naming the object to save this plot as "plot1".
- Object names cannot have spaces in them and cannot start with a number.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.code130[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.asp=.618, fig.retina=3, out.width='70%', fig.width=5}
## GGplot Step 1: Map the Tidy Data
plot1 <- ggplot(data = mpg, aes(x = drv))

plot1
```
]

???

By running the name of the new object `plot1` we can see how ggplot has set up the new visualization.

- Here we see a blank plot with room reserved across the x-axis for the three levels of the `drv` variable.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.code100[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='55%', fig.asp=.8, fig.width=4}
## GGplot Step 1: Map the Tidy Data
plot1 <- ggplot(data = mpg, aes(x = drv))

## GGplot Step 2: Add a geom
plot1 + geom_bar()
```
]

???

Step 2 we add a tool (geom) to the plot.

- ggplot works using `+` like you are adding functions together.

<br>

### Everybody have this plot made?

### Understand the logic of the code?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size45[.content-box-blue[**Categorical Variable: Make a Bar Plot**]]

<br>

<br>

.size45[**In R** make a bar plot of parties (`party`) that have controlled the presidency since 1953 in the `presidential` data set.]]

???

Let's practice!

- Aim for a count here NOT the proportions like we did last class.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.code100[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='55%', fig.asp=.8, fig.width=4}
## GGplot Step 1: Map the Tidy Data
plot2 <- ggplot(data = presidential, aes(x = party))

## GGplot Step 2: Add a geom
plot2 + geom_bar()
```
]

???

### Any questions on making simple bar plots?

<br>

### What kinds of visualizations can we make with a numeric variable?
- (Histograms and box plots!)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[.content-box-blue[**Numerical Variable: Make a Histogram**]]]

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '70%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/03_1-Johnson-Histogram.png")
```

???

### Remind me, what is a histogram? How is it like a bar plot?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size45[.content-box-blue[**Numerical Variable: Make a Histogram**]]

<br>

.size45[**In R** make a histogram of city fuel economy (`cty`) in the `mpg` data set.

Note: Use geom_histogram()
]]

???

### Everybody use the basic logic but the new geom to make a histogram of city fuel economy in the mpg data set.

- (SLIDE)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.code100[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='55%', fig.asp=.8, fig.width=4}
## GGplot Step 1: Map the Tidy Data
plot3 <- ggplot(data = mpg, aes(x = cty))

## GGplot Step 2: Add a geom
plot3 + geom_histogram()
```
]

???

Defaults to 30 bins, which is a lot!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.pull-left[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='95%', fig.asp=1, fig.width=4}
# Adjust the bins
plot3 + geom_histogram(bins = 5)
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='95%', fig.asp=1, fig.width=4}
# Adjust the bins
plot3 + geom_histogram(bins = 10)
```
]



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[.content-box-blue[**Numerical Variable: Make a Box Plot**]]]

<br>

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '50%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/03_1-Johnson-Box_plot.png")
```

???

### Remind me, what is a box plot? How is it like a histogram?





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size45[.content-box-blue[**Numerical Variable: Make a Box Plot**]]

<br>

.size45[**In R** make a box plot of city fuel economy (`cty`) in the `mpg` data set.

Note: Use geom_boxplot()
]]

???



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.code100[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='55%', fig.asp=.8, fig.width=4}
## GGplot Step 1: Map the Tidy Data
plot4 <- ggplot(data = mpg, aes(x = cty))

## GGplot Step 2: Add a geom
plot4 + geom_boxplot()
```
]



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size40[.content-box-blue[**Numerical Variables: Histograms and Box Plots**]]] 

.pull-left[
```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='100%', fig.asp=1, fig.width=4}
# Adjust the bins
plot3 + geom_histogram(bins = 10)
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='100%', fig.asp=1, fig.width=4}
## GGplot Step 1: Map the Tidy Data
plot4 <- ggplot(data = mpg, aes(x = cty))

## GGplot Step 2: Add a geom
plot4 + geom_boxplot()
```

]

???

### Compare and contrast




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size35[.content-box-blue[**Numerical Variable: Make a Histogram and Box Plot**]]

<br>

<br>

.size45[**In R** make a histogram AND box plot of the proportion of people that are college educated (`percollege`) in midwest cities (`midwest` data set).]]

???



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.pull-left[
.code80[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='100%', fig.asp=.8, fig.width=4}
## GGplot Step 1: Map the Tidy Data
p5 <- ggplot(data = midwest, aes(x = percollege))

## GGplot Step 2: Add a geom
p5 + geom_histogram(bins = 25)
```
]
]

.pull-right[
.code80[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='100%', fig.asp=.8, fig.width=4}
## GGplot Step 1: Map the Tidy Data
p5 <- ggplot(data = midwest, aes(x = percollege))

## GGplot Step 2: Add a geom
p5 + geom_boxplot()
```
]
]

???

### What do we learn from this data about the cities in the midwest?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size40[.content-box-blue[**Numeric Variables Across Time: Line Plots**]]]

<br>

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '70%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/03_1-Johnson-Line_plot.png")
```

???

### Refresh my memory, what does a line plot show us?






---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size45[.content-box-blue[**Numeric Variables Across Time: Line Plots**]]

<br>

.size45[**In R** make a line plot of the US population (`pop`) across time (`date`) using the `economics` data set.]]

.size45[
- Use geom_line()

- You must specify an X and a Y variable!
]

???




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.code130[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='47%', fig.asp=.8, fig.width=4}
## GGplot Step 1: Map the Tidy Data
plot6 <- ggplot(data = economics, aes(x = date, y = pop))

## GGplot Step 2: Add a geom
plot6 + geom_line()
```
]

???

### Lessons from this about US population growth?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size45[.content-box-blue[**Principle 3: Variable Type Determines Tool**]]]

.size40[
**Univariate Visualizations**

+ If a **categorical** variable, make a **bar plot**

+ If a **numeric** variable, make a **box plot** or **histogram**

+ If a **numeric variable across time**, make a **line plot**
]

???

### Questions on making these plots in R?

<br>

**SLIDE**: Let's talk outputting the viz you make in R for copying into a Word Doc.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '80%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/04_3-export_image1.png")
```

???

Three useful options through the export menu.

- Comes down to your preference.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '80%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/04_3-export_image2.png")
```

???

If saving as an image it defaults to png.

- You can then click and drag the size of this window to adjust the dimensions of the image.

<br>

Everybody open a word document and try using all three options to move an image into it from RStudio.

- Let's see if we can help each other figure this out!

<br>

**SLIDE**: So, you're working from home and you get stuck, what should you do?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.pull-left[

<br>

.size40[
+ Recipes 3.3 for bar plots

+ Recipe 6.1 for histograms

+ Recipe 6.6 for box plots

+ Recipe 4.1 for line Plots

+ Recipe 10.26 for outputting visualizations
]]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '90%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/04_3-R_Graphics_Cookbook.jpg")
```
]

???

SUPER useful reference book.

- Freely available online

- Laid out like a cookbook.
    - Each section is a recipe designed to help you solve a specific problem.

- I've linked to some very helpful ones throughout our syllabus.

<br>

Something not working? Notes incomplete?

- Try the cookbook!

<br>

Or Google it!

- Tons of great answers on using R out there.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.pull-left[
.code80[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='100%', fig.asp=.8, fig.width=4}
# Split the Two Steps
p2 <- ggplot(data = presidential, aes(x = party))

p2 + geom_bar()
```
]
]

.pull-right[
.code80[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='100%', fig.asp=.8, fig.width=4}
# Combine the two steps

ggplot(data = presidential, aes(x = party)) + 
  geom_bar()
```
]
]

???

One note so you don't get confused when using the cookbook.

- Winston Chang makes visualizations in one step, not two like we've been doing today.

<br>

My advice is to stick with the two separate steps until you are super comfortable in R.

- If you accidentally end a line of code with '+' and don't add a geom it can break all the code below it!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size65[**For Next Class**]

.size45[
1. Huntington-Klein 2022 chapter 3 "Describing Variables"

2. Review the Chang (2018) recipes on polishing a visualization
    - Recipe 3.4 (bar plot colors)
    - Recipes in Section 9 (and so much more!)
]

???

Keep practicing with RStudio!

- Make sure you're comfortable with all of today's code




### Questions on the assignment?
